(Dutch pronunciation: [[ɣɛŋk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")) is a [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Belgium "Municipalities of Belgium") and [city](/wiki/City_status_in_Belgium "City status in Belgium")[[2]](#cite_note-2) located in the Belgian [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") near [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt"). The municipality comprises only the town of Genk itself. It is one of the most important industrial towns in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), located on the [Albert Canal](/wiki/Albert_Canal "Albert Canal"), between [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") and [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège").

**Genk** (Dutch pronunciation: [[ɣɛŋk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")) is a [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Belgium "Municipalities of Belgium") and [city](/wiki/City_status_in_Belgium "City status in Belgium")[[2]](#cite_note-2) located in the Belgian [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") of [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") near [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt"). The municipality comprises only the town of Genk itself. It is one of the most important industrial towns in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), located on the [Albert Canal](/wiki/Albert_Canal "Albert Canal"), between [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") and [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Celtic and medieval origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Celtic and medieval origins")]

Genk probably originated as a [Celtic](/wiki/Celt "Celt") village, and was converted to Christianity in the 10th century. The remains of a little wooden church dating from that period were found in the area. The first mention of Genk as *Geneche* can be found in a document dating from 1108, ceding the territory to the Abbey of [Rolduc](/wiki/Rolduc "Rolduc"). Politically, Genk belonged to the [County of Loon](/wiki/County_of_Loon "County of Loon") until it was annexed by the [Prince-Bishopric of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège") in 1365.

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

During a century of on-going [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution#Belgium "Industrial Revolution") further south in Belgium, [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") modernised only slowly: Genk remained unimportant and small, growing slowly to a population of 2,000 around 1900. The village was the home of landscape painters and writers such as [Neel Doff](/wiki/Neel_Doff "Neel Doff") and became an artist colony where over 400 landscape painters painted en plein air.

### 20th-century development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th-century development")]

In 1901, [André Dumont](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Dumont_(geologist) "André Dumont (geologist)") found a large quantity of coal in the nearby village of [As](/wiki/As_(municipality) "As (municipality)"). Soon after, the "Black Gold" was also found in Genk. After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the village started to attract a large quantity of both Belgian and foreign immigrants, and quickly became the biggest town in Limburg after Hasselt, peaking to a population of 70,000. However, in 1966 the coal mine of Zwartberg closed down, and Genk had to develop new industries, mainly along the [Albert Canal](/wiki/Albert_Canal "Albert Canal") and highways. By the end of the 1980s, the two remaining coal mines at Winterslag and Waterschei were also closed.

In 2000, Genk officially became a city.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Genk_KoolmijnWinterslag_Schacht-Ontvangstgeb.jpg/330px-Genk_KoolmijnWinterslag_Schacht-Ontvangstgeb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Genk_KoolmijnWinterslag_Schacht-Ontvangstgeb.jpg)

Headstock at the former Winterslag Colliery

Genk is the industrial centre of the province of Limburg and offers over 45,000 jobs, making it economically the third most significant city in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1900, Genk was a quiet village with around 2,000 residents. At that time, Genk was known for its natural environment, popular among artists and painters who used Genk as a setting for their pieces. In 1901, coal was discovered in Genk and three mining sites were developed: Zwartberg, Waterschei and Winterslag (C-mine today). As a result, the population grew exponentially: today Genk has about 65,000 inhabitants with 107 different ethnic backgrounds. The mines had some good years, but in 1966 the Zwartberg mine closed, followed by Winterslag (C-mine today) in 1986 and Waterschei in 1987.

The [Genk Body & Assembly](/wiki/Genk_Body_%26_Assembly "Genk Body & Assembly") factory of [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company") was the largest and most important employer in Genk until recently, employing some 5,000 people and building the [Mondeo](/wiki/Ford_Mondeo "Ford Mondeo") sedan and hatchback, the [Galaxy](/wiki/Ford_Galaxy#Second_generation_Galaxy_.282006_to_present.29 "Ford Galaxy") (second generation, from 2006 and onwards) MPV/minivan, and the [S-MAX](/wiki/Ford_S-MAX "Ford S-MAX") sub-MPV. The factory closed in 2014.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### C-Mine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: C-Mine")]

The city went looking for new opportunities for the enormous mine sites on its territory, including the Winterslag site. In 2000, the idea began to grow to accommodate a creative hub in the buildings of the old coal mine of Winterslag. In 2001, the city of Genk bought the site from LRM (Limburgse Reconversie Maatschappij) and the name "C-mine" was born in 2005. As regards content, the creative hub works on four cornerstones: education, creative economy, creative recreation and artistic creation and presentation. With a university college specialised in various artistic graduation subjects, an incubator for young entrepreneurs, a cultural centre, a design centre, a cinema, C-mine expedition, etc. the C-mine mission has succeeded. It has created 330 jobs in 42 companies and organisations, including around 200 jobs in the creative sector in 33 creative companies.

Each day, C-mine produces: games, apps, websites, sets for television, drones, light shows, design items, stage productions, etc. C-mine is a site of creative makers in each of the four segments.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Genk_-_Domein_Bokrijk_Kasteel.jpg/250px-Genk_-_Domein_Bokrijk_Kasteel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Genk_-_Domein_Bokrijk_Kasteel.jpg)

Chateau of the Bokrijk Estate

* The biggest tourist attraction of Genk is [Bokrijk](/wiki/Bokrijk "Bokrijk"), an [open-air museum](/wiki/Open-air_museum "Open-air museum") consisting of authentic relocated buildings (mainly dating from 17th till 19th century) from all over [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"). In the summer season, historical Flanders comes alive in Bokrijk through numerous actors and [re-enactment](/wiki/Historical_reenactment "Historical reenactment") events.
* Genk was established as one of the entrance "gateways" of the [Hoge Kempen National Park](/wiki/Hoge_Kempen_National_Park "Hoge Kempen National Park"), the first [National Park](/wiki/National_Park "National Park") in Flanders, at its opening in 2006.
* Also noteworthy are the old coalmines of Zwartberg, Waterschei and Winterslag, surrounded by slag heaps, huge black mountains of dug up soil and coal remnants. Some of the mine buildings and housing can be visited.
* Despite its industrial past and present, Genk is nicknamed 'De Groene Stad' (*The Green City*). It sports a nature reserve called "De Maten", the large recreational area Kattevennen (with the Europlaneterium), Bokrijk, and several other green areas. In sunny weather, you may also want to pay a visit to the *[Sundial](/wiki/Sundial "Sundial") Park* ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Zonnewijzerpark*). The history of the landscape painters who visited Genk between 1840 and 1940 can be discovered in the Museum Emile Van Doren.
* The [Europlanetarium Genk](/wiki/Europlanetarium_Genk "Europlanetarium Genk") has a [planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium "Planetarium") and observatory.

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Events")]

* Genk was home of *Motives Festival*, an annual event taking place in November and celebrating "new sounds of jazz." Recent performers have included the fiery piano jazz of [Esbjörn Svensson Trio](/wiki/Esbj%C3%B6rn_Svensson_Trio "Esbjörn Svensson Trio"), funky saxman [Joshua Redman](/wiki/Joshua_Redman "Joshua Redman"), and futuristic electronics wizard [Leafcutter John](/wiki/Leafcutter_John "Leafcutter John"). Another musical event, *Genk on Stage*, takes place during three days in the summer. This festival is celebrated no more.
* Genk is also rich in tradition, with a colourful [carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") taking place around [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), the May celebrations featuring the [May Queen](/wiki/May_Queen "May Queen"), a flowers parade and a huge [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks") finale, and finally the Saint Martin [procession](/wiki/Procession "Procession"), in honour of Saint [Martin of Tours](/wiki/Martin_of_Tours "Martin of Tours"), one of the most popular saints in Flanders.
* In 2012 Genk was host to [Manifesta](/wiki/Manifesta "Manifesta"), The roving European Biennial of Contemporary Art, together with events such as the biennial of Venice and the Documenta in Kassel, Manifesta is one of the foremost art events of Europe.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Besides the Albert Canal, Genk has rail service to Hasselt and a small airport ([EBZW](/wiki/Zwartberg_Airfield "Zwartberg Airfield")) 6 km (3.7 mi) northeast of the town center. [De Lijn](/wiki/De_Lijn "De Lijn") is the sole bus transport service provider within Genk.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Cristal_Arena_2013-02-21_opposite_stand.jpg/250px-Cristal_Arena_2013-02-21_opposite_stand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristal_Arena_2013-02-21_opposite_stand.jpg)

[Luminus Arena](/wiki/Luminus_Arena "Luminus Arena")

Genk's major [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club, [KRC Genk](/wiki/K.R.C._Genk "K.R.C. Genk"), promoted from the second division in 1996 and quickly became one of Belgium's top clubs. They finished first in the [highest football league](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League") in 1999, 2002, 2011 and 2019 and won the [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") in 1998, 2000, 2009, 2013 and 2021. Because of this success, the *[Luminus Arena](/wiki/Luminus_Arena "Luminus Arena")* (formerly known as *[Fenix Stadium](/w/index.php?title=Fenix_Stadium&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fenix Stadium (page does not exist)")* and *[Cristal Arena](/wiki/Cristal_Arena "Cristal Arena")*) of KRC Genk (place for 25 500 supporters) has become an important centre of both sport and non-sport activities in recent years. In the 2016–2017 season Genk reached the quarter-finals of the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") by defeating fellow Belgian side [K.A.A. Gent](/wiki/K.A.A._Gent "K.A.A. Gent") over two legs, one of which was a 5–2 win.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Karting Genk](/wiki/Karting_Genk "Karting Genk") is a [kart racing](/wiki/Kart_racing "Kart racing") [CIK FIA](/wiki/CIK_FIA "CIK FIA") track. In 2011 it hosted the [Karting World Championship](/wiki/Karting_World_Championship "Karting World Championship").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Genk&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Demography")]

24% of the inhabitants are of foreign origin from about 85 different nationalities, mostly Italians, [Turks](/wiki/Turks_in_Belgium "Turks in Belgium"), and [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks_in_Belgium "Greeks in Belgium").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is believed that Genk has the largest Turkish community in Belgium relative to its population.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

* [Neel Doff](/wiki/Neel_Doff "Neel Doff"), writer (1858–1942)

* [Peter Vanhoutte](/w/index.php?title=Peter_Vanhoutte&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peter Vanhoutte (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Vanhoutte "nl:Peter Vanhoutte")], former politician and former [Belgian MP](/wiki/Belgian_MP "Belgian MP") (b. 1956)
* [Martin Margiela](/wiki/Martin_Margiela "Martin Margiela"), fashion designer (b. 1957)
* [Jo Vandeurzen](/wiki/Jo_Vandeurzen "Jo Vandeurzen"), politician and [Minister of Health](/wiki/Minister_of_Health "Minister of Health") in the [Flemish region](/wiki/Flemish_region "Flemish region") (b. 1958)
* [Dirk Medved](/wiki/Dirk_Medved "Dirk Medved"), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [defender](/wiki/Defender_(football) "Defender (football)") (b. 1968)
* [Ronny Gaspercic](/wiki/Ronny_Gaspercic "Ronny Gaspercic"), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [goalkeeper](/wiki/Goalkeeper_(football) "Goalkeeper (football)") (b. 1969)
* [Karel Geraerts](/wiki/Karel_Geraerts "Karel Geraerts"), [footballer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (b. 1982)
* [Benjamin De Ceulaer](/wiki/Benjamin_De_Ceulaer "Benjamin De Ceulaer"), [footballer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (b. 1983)
* [Siglo XX](/wiki/Siglo_XX_(band) "Siglo XX (band)"), 1980's [Coldwave](/wiki/Coldwave "Coldwave") band
* [Claudio Desolati](/wiki/Claudio_Desolati "Claudio Desolati"), Italian [footballer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (b. 1955)
